15

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.

126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45

179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction is a single \(\mathbb{N}\)-recursion (modelled on Mathlib's
193	\texttt{InjectiveResolution.ofCocomplex}) with no sorry-free partial fragment when
194	attempted wholesale. We therefore isolate its independent moving parts as the
195	sub-lemmas below; the target horseshoe lemma is then assembled from them. Throughout,
196	\(I_A : \mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\,A\) and \(I_C : \mathrm{InjectiveResolution}\,C\)
197	are fixed (they exist because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
198	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
199	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
200	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
201	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
202	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
203	
204	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
205	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
206	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
207	  \mathlibok
208	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
209	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
210	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
211	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
212	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
213	  resolutions.
214	\end{lemma}
215	
216	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
217	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
218	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
222	  \[
223	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
224	  \]
225	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
226	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
227	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
228	  every degree
229	  \[
230	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
231	  \]
232	  as split, supplying exactly the degreewise-splitting data the downstream comparison
233	  (\(\mathrm{rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES}\)) consumes.
234	\end{lemma}

236	\begin{lemma}[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
237	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
238	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
239	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
240	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
241	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
242	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
243	  \[
244	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
245	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
246	  \]
247	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
248	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
249	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
250	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
251	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
252	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
253	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
254	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	\begin{proof}
258	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
259	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
260	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
261	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
262	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
263	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
264	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
265	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
266	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
267	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
268	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
269	\end{proof}
270	
271	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
272	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
273	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_twist}
274	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
275	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
276	  \[
277	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
278	  \]
279	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
280	  identity
281	  \[
282	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
283	  \]
284	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
285	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
286	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives
287	  (\(\mathrm{Injective.factorThru}\)): the target summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and
288	  the relevant source map is a monomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
289	\end{lemma}
290	
291	\begin{proof}
292	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
293	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
294	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
295	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\) (this is
296	  \(\mathrm{factorThru}\)); the second component is \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
297	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
298	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
299	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
300	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
301	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\): precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
302	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
303	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\)
304	  (exactness of \(I_C\) at degree \(n\),
305	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). Hence
306	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
307	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
308	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
309	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
310	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
311	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
312	\end{proof}

314	\begin{lemma}[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
315	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
316	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_dComp}
317	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
318	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
319	  differential degreewise by the matrix
320	  \[
321	    d_B^n \;=\;
322	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
323	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
324	  \]
325	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
326	  cochain complex.
327	\end{lemma}

329	\begin{proof}
330	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
331	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
332	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
333	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
334	  \[
335	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
336	  \]
337	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
338	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
339	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
340	\end{proof}

342	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
343	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
344	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_chainMap}
345	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
346	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
347	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
348	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
349	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
350	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
351	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
352	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
353	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
354	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
355	\end{lemma}
356	
357	\begin{proof}
358	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
359	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
360	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
361	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
362	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
363	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
364	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
365	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
366	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
367	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
368	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
369	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
370	\end{proof}

372	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]

377	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
378	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
379	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
380	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).

385	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}

387	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
388	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
389	  cochain complexes
390	  \[
391	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
392	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .

395	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),

397	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
398	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for

403	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves

469	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
470	complex-level homology long exact sequence

473	sub-lemma below.

524	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
525	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
526	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift

529	\begin{proof}
530	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
531	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
532	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
533	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
534	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
535	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
536	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
537	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
538	
539	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
540	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
541	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
542	  complexes
543	  \[
544	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
545	  \]
546	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
547	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
548	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
549	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
551	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
552	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
553	  \[
554	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
555	      \longrightarrow 0
556	  \]
557	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
558	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
559	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
560	  \[
561	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
562	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
563	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
564	  \]
565	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
566	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
567	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
568	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
569	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
570	  sequence of right-derived functors
571	  \[
572	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
573	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
574	  \]
575	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
576	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
577	  \[
578	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
579	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
580	  \]
581	
582	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
583	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
584	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
585	  \[
586	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
587	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
588	  \]
589	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
590	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
591	  In the lowest degree the segment
592	  \[
593	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
594	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
595	  \]
596	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
597	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
598	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
599	  part (2).
600	\end{proof}

193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.

202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}

233	\begin{lemma}[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
234	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
235	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
236	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
237	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
238	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
239	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
240	  \[
241	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
242	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
243	  \]
244	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
245	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
246	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
247	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
248	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
249	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
250	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
251	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
252	\end{lemma}

256	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see

258	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
